title: Research
text: Our lab investigates how fundamental brain processes shape mental health across development. Using Electroencephalography (EEG) alongside behavioral tasks, clinical assessments, self-report measures, and computational modelling, we study populations ranging from typically developing children to individuals with or at risk for autism spectrum conditions, psychosis, and related neurodevelopmental disorders. The long-term goal is to advance a developmental, mechanism-based framework of mental health that supports earlier identification of risk and contributes to more effective, individualized care.

title: Research
text:
Research Themes




Sensory Processing Dynamics
Examining how individual differences in sensory sensitivity and information processing shape neurodevelopmental pathways and confer vulnerability or resilience.
Learn more →





Neurobiological Markers of Vulnerability
Identifying and validating objective neurocognitive indicators for early detection and biologically informed characterization of mental health risk.
Learn more →





Mechanistic Modelling
Developing integrative and computational frameworks linking sensory and cognitive processes to symptom dimensions and broader mental health outcomes.
Learn more →

title: Research
text:
Sensory Processing Dynamics
The way the brain registers, filters, and prioritises sensory information varies considerably across individuals and these differences are not trivial. Atypical sensory responsivity is among the earliest observable features of neurodevelopmental conditions such as Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and has increasingly been linked to broader mental health outcomes including anxiety and affective dysregulation.
Our work in this theme uses Electroencephalography (EEG) to capture the millisecond-level temporal dynamics of sensory processing. By examining Event-Related Potentials (ERPs) such as the P1/N1 complex, the mismatch negativity (MMN), and the N2/P3 we study how the brain detects, habituates to, discriminates, and evaluates between sensory inputs across development. We investigate these processes in both typically developing populations and clinical groups, asking how individual differences in sensory gating, sensory habituation, and evaluation relate to neurodevelopmental trajectories.
A key question is whether altered sensory processing is a vulnerability pathway, conferring risk for downstream difficulties in cognitive control, emotion regulation, and social functioning, or whether, under supportive conditions, heightened sensory sensitivity may also confer adaptive advantages. By tracking these processes longitudinally and across diagnoses, we aim to build a dimensional account of sensory responsivity that cuts across traditional diagnostic boundaries.
View publications on this theme →

title: Research
text:
Neurobiological Markers of Vulnerability
Early identification of mental health risk remains a major challenge, in part because current diagnostic systems rely heavily on behavioural symptoms that often emerge only after prolonged periods of difficulty. This theme focuses on identifying objective, neurophysiological indicators that can detect vulnerability before the full clinical picture has developed.
We use EEG-derived measures, including the P1/N1, mismatch negativity (MMN), N2, P3, and oscillatory markers such as theta synchrony and alpha power, as candidate biomarkers of neurocognitive vulnerability. These components reflect fundamental brain processes such as prediction error signalling, attentional resource allocation, and neural synchronisation, and have been shown to be altered in individuals at clinical high risk for psychosis, in ASD, and across other conditions.
Crucially, we adopt a transdiagnostic approach. Rather than searching for markers specific to a single disorder, we investigate which electrophysiological signatures index shared mechanisms of risk that cut across conditions such as ASD, ADHD, and schizophrenia. By applying identical paradigms across clinical and non-clinical populations at different developmental stages, we aim to map how these markers emerge, change, and predict outcomes over time, ultimately supporting earlier, biologically grounded identification of those who may benefit most from preventive support.
View publications on this theme →

title: Research
text:
Mechanistic Modelling
Establishing that a brain signal differs between groups is an important first step, but it does not explain how or why that difference arises or how it relates to the lived experience of symptoms. This theme aims to bridge that gap by developing integrative and computational frameworks that formally link sensory and cognitive processes to mental health outcomes.
Drawing on approaches from computational psychiatry and psychology, we use model-based analysis to move beyond group-level comparisons toward mechanistic accounts of individual variation. For example, predictive coding frameworks allow us to model how the brain combines prior expectations with incoming sensory evidence, and how disruptions in the precision-weighting of these signals may give rise to atypical perception, inflexible behaviour, or heightened distress. By fitting such models to EEG data, we can quantify latent cognitive processes that standard ERP analysis alone cannot capture.
A central goal is to connect these computational parameters to dimensional symptom profiles rather than categorical diagnoses. This means asking not “does group A differ from group B?” but “which parameters of sensory-cognitive processing predict where an individual falls on continuous dimensions of, for example, sensory sensitivity, anxiety, or social withdrawal?” In doing so, we work toward a mechanistic, person-level framework for understanding mental health, one that can inform both theory and the development of targeted, individualised interventions.
View publications on this theme →

title: Research
text:
Methods
Our research is mostly anchored in scalp-recorded Electroencephalography (EEG), which offers millisecond-level temporal resolution for tracking how the brain processes information in real time. The lab draws on a range of analytic approaches including Event-Related Potentials (ERPs), time-frequency decomposition, functional connectivity, and computational modelling used alongside behavioral tasks, clinical assessments, and self-report measures. Together, these approaches give us the temporal precision and analytic flexibility needed to link brain dynamics to behavior and mental health across development.







Tip



Interested in collaborating, or in our research? See our publications, or get in touch.

title: Join the Lab
text: We welcome motivated researchers and students who are passionate about understanding how the brain shapes mental health. We value curiosity, rigour, and collaborative work.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Open Positions
There are no funded PhD or postdoc positions currently available. However, we are always interested in hearing from strong candidates. If you are considering applying for external fellowship funding and your research interests overlap with ours, please get in touch — see Postdoctoral Opportunities below.

title: Join the Lab
text:
MSc Thesis Projects
We regularly supervise MSc thesis students. Currently this is open to students from Maastricht University only.
How to apply: Send an email with: (1) brief motivation (half page), (2) your CV, and (3) your transcript. Include “MSc Thesis Inquiry” in the subject line.
📧 Email NeuroMH Lab
Timeline: Applications reviewed on a rolling basis.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Postdoctoral Opportunities
We do not currently have funded postdoctoral openings, but we welcome inquiries from researchers interested in applying for fellowship funding together with the lab. Relevant schemes include:

NWO Veni (Netherlands)
Marie Skłodowska-Curie Postdoctoral Fellowship (EU)

If your research interests match ours and you are interested in applying for one of these, please get in touch with a CV and a brief research statement.

title: Join the Lab
section: What to Expect in Our Lab
text:
What to Expect in Our Lab


🔬 Rigorous Science
We maintain high standards for study design, analysis, and reporting.


👥 Collaborative Culture
Regular lab meetings, one-on-one supervision, and a supportive team environment.


🌍 International Network
Connections with collaborators across the Netherlands, Europe and beyond.

title: NeuroMH Lab
text:
Department of Cognitive Neuroscience
Maastricht University
We combine experimental neuroscience with clinical research to examine how core systems such as sensory responsivity, memory, and cognitive control interact over time to influence adaptive and maladaptive health outcomes.
Using non-invasive neuroimaging, primarily Electroencephalography (EEG) in combination with behavioral paradigms, and validated clinical and self-report measures, we capture the fine-grained temporal dynamics of neural information processing.
A central aim is to move beyond symptom-based classification toward transdiagnostic, dimensional models of mental health.
Read our full mission →

title: Neurocognitive Development & Mental Health Research Lab
text: Mental health problems often emerge during development, yet the brain mechanisms underlying vulnerability or resilience remain insufficiently understood. The Neurocognitive Development & Mental Health Research Lab investigates the fundamental neurocognitive processes that shape mental health trajectories across the lifespan.

title: Neurocognitive Development & Mental Health Research Lab
text: Mental health problems often emerge during development, yet the brain mechanisms underlying vulnerability or resilience remain insufficiently understood. The Neurocognitive Development & Mental Health Research Lab investigates the fundamental neurocognitive processes that shape mental health trajectories across the lifespan.

title: Neurocognitive Development & Mental Health Research Lab
text:
Approach
We combine experimental neuroscience with clinical research to examine how core systems such as sensory responsivity, memory, and cognitive control interact over time to influence adaptive and maladaptive outcomes.
Using non-invasive neuroimaging, primarily Electroencephalography (EEG), in combination with behavioral paradigms and validated clinical and self-report measures, we capture the fine-grained temporal dynamics of neural information processing.
Through Event-Related Potentials (ERPs), time-frequency analyses, and functional connectivity approaches, we characterize how brain networks develop and reorganize from childhood into adulthood, giving rise to individual differences in cognition and behavior.

title: Neurocognitive Development & Mental Health Research Lab
text:
Framework
A central aim of our work is to move beyond symptom-based classification toward transdiagnostic, dimensional models of mental health. By applying identical experimental frameworks across typically developing and clinical populations, including Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and schizophrenia, we identify neurocognitive mechanisms that explain heterogeneity within and across diagnoses.
Rather than treating disorders as fixed categories, we focus on processes that cut across traditional diagnostic boundaries.

title: Neurocognitive Development & Mental Health Research Lab
text:
Long-Term Goal
Our long-term goal is to translate these insights into objective neurobiological markers that support early risk detection and stratification of vulnerability.
By linking sensory and cognitive systems to developmental trajectories, we aim to inform prevention and intervention strategies tailored to individual neurocognitive profiles.
Ultimately, the goal of our research is to advance a developmental, mechanism-based framework of mental health that supports earlier identification of risk and contributes to more effective, individualized care.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching at Maastricht University is grounded in Problem-Based Learning (PBL) and active engagement with real research problems. Courses are designed to help students build not just content knowledge but the critical thinking and methodological skills needed for independent scientific work, whether in the lab, the clinic, or beyond.
Franc holds a University Teaching Qualification (UTQ), is PBL-certified, and successfully completed the FPN mentor training, and the Maastricht University’s Inspiring Educational Leadership Trajectory (IEL). His courses and lectures have received high student satisfaction ratings over the past years.

Franc holds a University Teaching Qualification (UTQ), is PBL-certified, and successfully completed the FPN mentor training, and the Maastricht University’s Inspiring Educational Leadership Trajectory (IEL). His courses and lectures have received high student satisfaction ratings over the past years.

title: Teaching
text:
Bachelor’s Courses

Lifespan Psychology
Programme: BSc Psychology
Period: Period 5 (April-June)
Level: Year 1
Students: Undergraduate psychology students (~450 per year)
Role: Course Coordinator
The course provides an in-depth analysis of critical developmental processes including perceptual, cognitive, linguistic, emotional, social, and motor development. Key clinical conditions, including ADHD, schizophrenia, obsessive-compulsive disorder, depression, and dementia are examined as well including psychological interventions, pharmacological treatments, and other emerging therapeutic modalities. By integrating developmental and clinical perspectives, the course provides a well-rounded view of human psychological development, variation within it, and potential paths for intervention.

title: Teaching
text:
Master’s Courses

Development of Cognition and Language
Programme: MSc Psychology
Period: Period 2 (October-December)
Level: Master’s
Students: Graduate psychology students (50–80 per year)
Role: Course Coordinator
This course explores the typical and atypical development (dyslexia, dyscalculia) of higher-order cognitive processes that underlie learning and academic achievement. Students examine how core cognitive systems, including working memory, episodic and semantic memory, language, numeracy and mathematical reasoning, executive functions, and general intelligence, develop and interact over time.

title: Teaching
text:
Workshops

The Assessment Cycle
Programme: University Teaching Qualification
Period: Period 2 & 4 (Oct & Mar)
Level: PhD
Participants: Teaching staff (15–20 per year)
Role: Workshop Coordinator
The UTQ programme supports staff as they develop and improve with regard to the competences needed for teaching at university, and guides them in obtaining a UTQ certificate. The Assessment Cycle workshop consists of 2 sessions of 2 hours. The workshops focus on assessment policy and vision, constructive alignment, and the application of provided guidelines with respect to different types of assessment.

title: Teaching
text:
Thesis Supervision
We regularly supervise Bachelor’s and Master’s thesis projects related to the lab’s research themes. Topics typically involve neurodevelopmental conditions, sensory processing sensitivity, and computational approaches to mental health, using EEG data and/or questionnaire-based data across healthy or clinical populations. To date, Franc has supervised over 60 Master’s thesis students and numerous Bachelor’s students at Maastricht University, and has served as (Co-)Promotor for four PhD candidates.






Tip



Interested in joining? See our open positions for current opportunities.

title: Teaching
text:

title: Lab Members
text:
Associate Professor · Department of Cognitive Neuroscience · Maastricht University
His research focuses on how fundamental sensory and cognitive brain processes shape mental health across development.
He received his PhD in Cognitive Neuroscience from Tilburg University and completed postdoctoral training at Duke University and the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, where he co-directed the Neurocognition and Imaging Research Lab and served as Managing Director of the Electrophysiology Research Core at the Carolina Institute for Developmental Disabilities. He has contributed to large-scale collaborative efforts including the North American Prodrome Longitudinal Study (NAPLS) consortium, and his work has been recognized with a Young Investigator Award from the Brain and Behavior Research Foundation and a Foundation of Hope Early Investigator Award.
At Maastricht, he leads research using EEG alongside behavioral tasks, clinical assessments, self-report measures, and computational modelling to identify neurobiological markers of vulnerability that may support earlier, more individualized approaches to mental health care. He also coordinates the Faculty’s Electroencephalography (EEG) laboratory infrastructure.

📧 Email · 🎓 Google Scholar · 🆔 ORCID · 🐙 GitHub
